name: Niobium
name_origin: Named after Niobe, daughter of Tantalus in Greek mythology, reflecting the element's close chemical similarity to tantalum. Previously known as 'columbium' in the United States until IUPAC standardized the name in 1949.

discovery.discoverer: Charles Hatchett (as columbium); identified as a new element by Heinrich Rose in 1844

occurrence.abundance_summary: Niobium is the 33rd most abundant element in Earth's crust at about 17 ppm. It occurs primarily in the mineral pyrochlore and in columbite-tantalite (coltan), almost always alongside tantalum. Brazil and Canada dominate world production.

compounds.name: Niobium pentoxide
compounds.summary: The principal oxide of niobium, used in capacitors, optical glasses, and as a precursor for niobium metal.

compounds.name: Niobium carbide
compounds.summary: An extremely hard refractory ceramic used in cutting tools and to refine grain in steels.

compounds.name: Niobium-tin
compounds.summary: An A15 superconducting intermetallic used in MRI and high-field research magnets.

compounds.name: Niobium pentachloride
compounds.summary: A yellow volatile solid used as a Lewis-acid catalyst and a precursor in niobium chemistry.

compounds.name: Lithium niobate
compounds.summary: A ferroelectric crystal widely used in nonlinear optics, electro-optic modulators, and surface acoustic wave devices.

uses: Microalloying additive in HSLA (high-strength low-alloy) steels for pipelines and structural steel
Superconducting magnets for MRI, particle accelerators, and tokamaks (Nb-Ti and Nb3Sn)
Superalloys for jet engines and rocket nozzles
Capacitors in electronics (niobium oxide and niobium-tantalum capacitors)
Surgical implants and jewelry due to biocompatibility and hypoallergenic properties
reactivity_summary: Niobium is a soft, ductile, lustrous gray transition metal that is highly resistant to corrosion thanks to a tenacious oxide layer. It is unattacked by most acids at room temperature, including aqua regia, but dissolves in hydrofluoric acid. At elevated temperatures it reacts with oxygen, halogens, sulfur, and nitrogen, and becomes superconducting below 9.2 K, the highest critical temperature of any pure element.
isotopes_summary: Niobium has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope, 93Nb (100% natural abundance), making it monoisotopic. About 30 radioisotopes have been characterized, with 92Nb (half-life 34.7 million years) being the longest-lived. Several short-lived niobium isotopes are produced as activation products in nuclear reactors.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
